focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...woman was still blotting at her streaming eyes between guests, still employed for at least the next five minutes of somebody else's emergency. On the half-landing he passed Freya carrying a crate of dessert spoons against her hip, limping openly now that she thought she was out of sight. She gave him one quick look at his empty lapel where the radio had been and understood enough not to ask. The dumbwaiter rattled. Somebody in the kitchen called for more beurre blanc. Upstairs a donor laughed with his whole throat. The night had not paused to let his life break in it.

In the mews the rain had started again, finer now but steadier, needling the black paint of the cars and drawing silver lines down the catering van doors. Beni stood under the weak yellow light by the bins and listened to the house behind him go on earning its own story. He had one hundred and seventy pounds less than silence used to be worth and no shift in the morning worth counting on. He had no key in his pocket for the side door, no next instruction, and no respectable sentence to put between himself and what he had done.

The practical disaster had already started. Laci would still want rent. Mercer would hear by morning. Eszter would not forgive him because unemployment was not virtue and confession did not make her housing safer retroactively. None of that changed because he had finally put objects on a table and said yes when asked a factual question.

Still, when he stepped out of the mews and into the wet Belgravia street, the air felt different in his chest, not cleaner, not lighter, but no longer rented. He turned away from the front door and walked, rain working into the Mercer wool at his shoulders, because walking was what remained once the car, the keys, and the house had been taken back.
